title: The hardware memory model and ordering
description: x86 is strongly ordered (TSO) while ARM/POWER are weakly ordered, so code that 'works' on x86 can break on ARM without explicit fences.
body: Use when porting concurrent code across ISAs or debugging rare ordering bugs. x86-64 uses total-store-order: loads are not reordered with loads, so many missing-fence bugs stay hidden; ARM64 and POWER allow aggressive reordering, exposing those bugs as field failures. Do not treat 'passes on my x86 laptop' as proof of correctness for ARM targets. Actions: reason in terms of acquire/release semantics, add the minimal correct fence, and test on the weakest target under stress. Validation signals include a clean TSan run and a passing stress test on ARM. Risk boundary: ordering bugs are probabilistic and hardware-specific; never dismiss one as 'cannot reproduce' without testing the weakly-ordered target.
